rationale: In the theory section, the cited study is explicitly summarized for its key finding that affective state changes the persuasiveness of gain/loss-framed mammography detection messages. The citing authors use that result, together with nearby evidence, to argue that detection contexts are affect-laden and to motivate their own fear-based account of detection behavior. Even without direct method reuse, this citation meaningfully informs the paper's conceptual setup and hypothesis framing.
relation_description: The citing paper relies on Keller et al. (2003) as prior empirical grounding that affect matters for detection-message persuasion. It uses this as part of the conceptual foundation for proposing and testing a fear-centered mechanism for detection versus prevention behaviors.
